items.name: Sequence
items.statement: Let $\mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ denote the natural numbers, i.e., the positive integers. A \textbf{sequence} (of real numbers) is a function from $\mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ to $\mathbb{R}$. If $a$ is a sequence, it is usual to write $a_n$ in place of $a(n)$, to call $a_n$ the $n$th term of the sequence, and to denote the sequence by $(a_n)$, or by $(a_n)_{n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}}$ if we wish for the index set to be explicit.

items.name: Bounded Sequence
items.statement: A sequence $(a_n)$ is said to be \textbf{bounded} if there exist an $M > 0$ such that $|a_n| \leq M$ for all $n$.

items.name: Limit of a Sequence
items.statement:
A sequence $(a_n)$ is said to have a \textbf{limit} $L$ if, for any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a number $N$ (depending on $\epsilon$) such that
$$|a_n - L| < \epsilon$$
for every integer $n \geq N$. If $(a_n)$ has a limit $L$, then we say that the sequence is \textbf{convergent} and that it \textbf{converges} to $L$ and we write $\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = L$. If $(a_n)$ is not convergent, it is said to \textbf{diverge} or to be \textbf{divergent}. If, for every $c > 0$, there exists a number $N$ such that $a_n > c$ for all $n \geq N$, we will write $\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = \infty$ and say that $(a_n)$ \textbf{diverges to} $\infty$. Similarly, if, for every $c > 0$, there exists a number $N$ such that $a_n < -c$ for all $n \geq N$, we will write $\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = -\infty$ and say that $(a_n)$ \textbf{diverges to} $-\infty$.
Every convergent sequence of real numbers is bounded.

items.name: Monotone Sequence
items.statement: A sequence $(a_n)$ is said to be \textbf{nondecreasing} if $a_m \leq a_n$ whenever $m < n$ and to be \textbf{strictly increasing} if $a_m < a_n$ whenever $m < n$. Similarly, the sequence is said to be \textbf{nonincreasing} if $a_m \geq a_n$ whenever $m < n$ and to be \textbf{strictly decreasing} if $a_m > a_n$ whenever $m < n$. Finally, $(a_n)$ is said to be \textbf{monotone} if it is either nondecreasing or nonincreasing.

items.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem
items.statement: Every bounded monotone sequence is convergent. More precisely, if $(a_n)$ is a bounded nondecreasing sequence then $\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = \sup\{a_n \mid n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}\}$.

items.name: Subsequence
items.statement: If $(a_n)$ is a sequence and if $(n_k)$ is a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers, then the sequence $(a_{n_k})$ is said to be a \textbf{subsequence} of $(a_n)$.
If $(a_{n_k})$ is a subsequence of a sequence $(a_n)$ and if $\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = L$, then $\lim_{k \to \infty} a_{n_k} = L$.

items.name: Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem
items.statement: Every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence.

Suppose that $(a_n)$ is a sequence and let $T$ denote the set of all limits of convergent subsequences.
(i) Suppose that $T \neq \emptyset$.
  (a) If $\sup T < \infty$, then $\sup T \in T$ and, if $\inf T > -\infty$, then $\inf T \in T$.
  (b) If $\sup T = \infty$, there is a subsequence of $(a_n)$ diverging to $\infty$ and, if $\inf T = -\infty$, there is a subsequence of $(a_n)$ diverging to $-\infty$.
(ii) If $T = \emptyset$ then no subsequence of $(a_n)$ converges and there is a subsequence diverging to $\infty$ or to $-\infty$ (it is possible that some subsequences of $(a_n)$ diverge to $\infty$ and others diverge to $-\infty$).

items.name: Limit Superior and Limit Inferior
items.statement:
Suppose that $(a_n)$ is a sequence and let $T$ be the set of all limits of convergent subsequences.
(i) The \textbf{limit superior} of the sequence $(a_n)$ is the element $\limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n$ of the set $\mathbb{R} \cup \{-\infty, \infty\}$ defined as follows:
  (a) If $(a_n)$ is not bounded above, then $\limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n = \infty$.
  (b) If $(a_n)$ is bounded above and if $T \neq \emptyset$, then $\limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n$ is the largest number that is the limit of a convergent subsequence of $(a_n)$.
  (c) If $(a_n)$ is bounded above and if $T = \emptyset$, then $\limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n = -\infty$.
(ii) The \textbf{limit inferior} of the sequence $(a_n)$ is the element $\liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n$ of the set $\mathbb{R} \cup \{-\infty, \infty\}$ defined as follows:
  (a) If $(a_n)$ is not bounded below, then $\liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n = -\infty$.
  (b) If $(a_n)$ is bounded below and if $T \neq \emptyset$, then $\liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n$ is the smallest number that is the limit of a convergent subsequence of $(a_n)$.
  (c) If $(a_n)$ is bounded below and if $T = \emptyset$, then $\liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n = \infty$.

Suppose that $(a_n)$ is a sequence.
(i) If the sequence $(a_n)$ converges to a number or diverges to $\pm\infty$ then
$$\liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n = \lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = \limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n.$$
(ii) If $\liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n = \lim_{n \to \infty} a_n$ then either the sequence $(a_n)$ converges or diverges to $\pm\infty$; in either case (2.1) holds.

Let $(a_n)$ be a sequence of numbers and let $L$ be a number.
(i) If $L = \limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n$ then
  (a) for each $\epsilon > 0$ the inequality $a_n > L + \epsilon$ holds for only finitely many $n$ and
  (b) for each $\epsilon > 0$ the inequality $a_n > L - \epsilon$ holds for infinitely many $n$.
(ii) If conditions (ia) and (ib) hold, then $L = \limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n$.

For any bounded sequence $(a_n)$ we have
$$\limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sup\{a_k \mid k \geq n\}$$
and
$$\liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n = \lim_{n \to \infty} \inf\{a_k \mid k \geq n\}.$$

items.name: Cauchy Sequence
items.statement: A sequence $(a_n)$ is said to be a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if, for every $\epsilon > 0$, there is an integer $N$ such that $|a_n - a_m| < \epsilon$ for all $n, m \geq N$.

items.name: Cauchy Criterion
items.statement: A sequence of real numbers is convergent if and only if it is a Cauchy sequence.

items.name: Convergence in R^d
items.statement:
A sequence $(\boldsymbol{x}_n)$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$ is said to have a \textbf{limit} $\boldsymbol{L}$, or to \textbf{converge to} $\boldsymbol{L}$ if, for any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists an integer $N$ such that
$$\|\boldsymbol{x}_n - \boldsymbol{L}\| < \epsilon$$
for every $n \geq N$, and in this case we will write $\lim_{n \to \infty} \boldsymbol{x}_n = \boldsymbol{L}$.

items.name: Component-wise Convergence
items.statement: If $(\boldsymbol{x}_n)$ is a sequence in $\mathbb{R}^d$ and if $\boldsymbol{L}$ is a point in $\mathbb{R}^d$, then $\lim_{n \to \infty} \boldsymbol{x}_n = \boldsymbol{L}$ if and only if $\lim_{n \to \infty} x_{n,i} = L_i$ for $i \in \{1, \ldots, d\}$.

items.name: Bounded Sequence in R^d
items.statement: A sequence $(\boldsymbol{x}_n)$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$ is said to be \textbf{bounded} if there exists a number $M > 0$ such that $\|\boldsymbol{x}_n\| \leq M$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$.

items.name: Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem in R^d
items.statement: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}^d$ has a convergent subsequence.

items.name: Contraction Mapping Theorem
items.statement: A contraction on $\mathbb{R}^d$ is continuous and has a unique fixed point.
